Hey, guys.
Hey, Eric, how's it going?
Too bad.

We've just got another meeting wrapped.
No worries.

Hi!
Sorry I'm late.
We were back to back.
No worries.
Yeah, it's great to meet you.
I was out of office last week, but my name's Aida.

I also set you up in Atlas.

I joined GrowthX in December, which back then we were only 15 people, I think, or 18.
So it's been a while here.
But before this, I was working at another content marketing agency called Animals.

I write for, if you're.
I Samrash, you've probably read some of my blog posts, and I still, I love freelance writing because I have to keep that muscle alive.
I refuse to use ChatGPT when I'm actually writing my freelance content because I do not want that muscle to deteriorate, so, yeah.
Nice.
That's awesome.
I'm glad to have you here in our corner, Aida.
Nice to meet you.
Yeah, it's great to meet you as well.

thank you.

Nice.

So I'm going to go ahead and share my screen.

So the artifacts that you read through, so we create them using publicly available information about your company.

And we also take the transcript from our meetings and we feed all of that to create them and make sure that they're as tailored and unique as possible because they're really important for the content generation process.
So I wanted to show you where they actually live and how they impact, um,

The article generation process end-to-end, so this is Atlas, which is our content generation platform, and right now we are in the context artifacts, and no, actually, we're not, we're in the assignment generation pipelines.

These are the context artifacts, so the three standard ones that everybody starts with are the audience personas, the company context, and the writing guidelines, and we can add additional artifacts depending on need, so let's say you, you we generate a calibration blog for you, and it turns out that the content kind of talks about a very, something very nuanced in a way that you really don't want it to talk about that, so we can create an additional artifact that, you know, reads an article, draft, draft, and then goes back and fixes those errors.

Or, you know, in very highly regulated industries, for example, we have some clients that are in healthcare, and we really need to make sure that there are some things that their content never mentions.
For example, there might not be allowed to refer to patients as consumers or customers.
So we might build a custom artifact that just contains instructions to read through the blog and replace all mentions of customers with patients and things like that.
So this is, you know, just the starting kit, but we can expand it as need arises.
And then I wanted to show you the article generation workflow.
So the usual, the two pipelines that we use, the starting ones are the assignment generation and the article generation.
Assignment generation is very simple, and it just allows us to upload a list of keywords or topics.
And then the tool.
We'll do an SEO analysis using SEMrush, and then it will generate assignment titles, briefs, which makes our jobs a whole lot easier because we don't have to do this manually.
And then the article generation one is much more complex.
So I already ran a keyword through this process today just so I can show you what it looks like.
But keep in mind, I didn't do any sort of manual editing, so the content isn't going to be as polished as it usually is.
So we start with a keyword or a title, and we usually give it an assignment direction.
This can be as basic as write a listicle, write a how-to guide, write, I don't know, a 10-step checklist, whatever it is.
And this can be very, very detailed.
We could include what to add.
But not to add, like, we can really shape the complete angle here.
Once this is ready, we proceed with the assignment brief.
At this stage, Atlas is going to use SEMrush to analyze the top-ranking content for that topic or the target keyword.

And it returns a brief that outlines who the target audience is, what is the SEO metadata, and it also analyzes the keyword, and it finds other related keyword opportunities, which helps us rank for more keywords.
At this stage, the company context and the audience personas come into play.
But the writing guidelines aren't applied until the article draft stage.
And then it kind of generates a proposed content structure.
We edit it pretty heavily.
And then when we're happy with, like, just the bare-bone content structure, we run the research step.
This research step, it uses, I believe...
Perplexity to research the topic in great detail.
So it uses the assignment brief and then it asks questions that it needs to get the answers to to actually write an article about this.
I think if you've ever tried to write an article using Chachi Biti or Claude and you didn't provide it any sort of research, the content can be pretty thin.
You know, the sections are all very, very short.
So that's what we're trying to avoid here.
And then when the research step is done, we run the outline generator.
And this is when it will, you know, provide a word count for each section.
And it contains bullet points of all of the key points that it's going to include.
We heavily edit this.
I don't think we've ever, we never really generate content without editing the outline.
We make sure, you know, if we have, for example.
Sometimes we might notice that a specific section could use a product mention, so we might, you know, say, like, mention logic here in this way.
And then when we're happy with the outline, we just generate the draft, and then we run the fact checker, the internal links, and then we will do another round of editing.

If we do the outline right, the last round of editing is usually a lot faster, but that's kind of our process end-to-end, and then we will take this document, paste it in a Google Docs, send it over to you, and then you review it.
So Atlas right now is an internal tool, so only we're using it, but we take all of your feedback, especially in the beginning, and we tailor the artifacts accordingly.
So, yeah, that's Atlas.
Do you have any questions?
This is pretty awesome.
Thank

mean, like, generally, how far up do we, like, do we enumerate, like, a month's worth of content?

recommendations?
